title: Association analysis — statistical models, correlation and group tests
text: association_analysis() downstream statistical engine semseeker. semseeker() computed epimutation counts, lesion counts delta metrics (DELTAS, DELTAR, ranked variants DELTAP/DELTARP DELTAQ/DELTARQ), function tests whether values associated phenotypic clinical variables. function supports three broad families models, run per genomic region parallel using future (Bengtsson 2021): Multiple comparisons adjusted Benjamini-Hochberg FDR procedure (Benjamini Hochberg 1995). analyses run across every combination genomic area, marker sample group defined semseeker() call.

title: Association analysis — statistical models, correlation and group tests
text: need completed semseeker run (see Getting started vignette). sample sheet used original semseeker() call must already contain clinical variable(s) want test:
code: # In practice: sample_sheet <- read.csv2("sample_sheet.csv"). # Here we use the synthetic sample sheet defined in the hidden setup chunk.  # Required: Sample_ID, Sample_Group # Plus any clinical covariates you plan to test: # age, bmi, treatment, exposure_level, ... head(sample_sheet) #>   Sample_ID Sample_Group age  bmi sex #> 1      S001    Reference  34 22.1   F #> 2      S002    Reference  41 25.4   M #> 3      S003    Reference  29 19.8   F #> 4      S004         Case  52 28.6   M #> 5      S005         Case  47 26.3   F #> 6      S006         Case  61 31.1   M

title: Association analysis — statistical models, correlation and group tests
text: Spearman (Spearman 1904) default recommendation SEM metrics often skewed non-normally distributed. Pearson assumes bivariate normality. Kendall’s tau (Kendall 1938) preferable many tied values.
code: # Spearman rank correlation (recommended for non-normal SEM metrics) inf <- data.frame(formula="DELTARP ~ age", family="spearman",                   transformation_y="none", depth_analysis=3,                   filter_p_value=TRUE)  # Pearson linear correlation inf <- data.frame(formula="DELTARP ~ bmi", family="pearson",                   transformation_y="log", depth_analysis=3,                   filter_p_value=FALSE)  # Kendall's tau (more robust with tied ranks) inf <- data.frame(formula="MUTATIONS_HYPO ~ age", family="kendall",                   transformation_y="none", depth_analysis=2,                   filter_p_value=TRUE)  # Spearman with permutation-based p-values (more accurate for small N) # Syntax: "spearman-permutation_<n_permutations>" inf <- data.frame(formula="DELTARP ~ age", family="spearman-permutation_1000",                   transformation_y="none", depth_analysis=3,                   filter_p_value=TRUE)  # Jensen-Shannon divergence (compares distributions) inf <- data.frame(formula="DELTARP ~ Sample_Group", family="jsd",                   transformation_y="none", depth_analysis=2,                   filter_p_value=TRUE)  # Run the analysis (uncomment when you have a real semseeker results folder): # association_analysis(inf, result_folder = "~/semseeker_results/")

title: Association analysis — statistical models, correlation and group tests
text: Wilcoxon rank-sum test (Wilcoxon 1945) recommended non-parametric alternative t-test normality assumption verified, common mutation count data.
code: # Wilcoxon rank-sum / Mann-Whitney (two independent groups) inf <- data.frame(formula="MUTATIONS_HYPO ~ Sample_Group", family="wilcoxon",                   transformation_y="none", depth_analysis=2,                   filter_p_value=TRUE)  # Paired Wilcoxon (repeated measures / matched samples) inf <- data.frame(formula="DELTARP ~ timepoint", family="wilcoxon.paired_timepoint",                   transformation_y="none", depth_analysis=2,                   filter_p_value=TRUE)  # Student's t-test inf <- data.frame(formula="DELTARP ~ Sample_Group", family="t.test",                   transformation_y="scale", depth_analysis=3,                   filter_p_value=TRUE)  # Paired t-test inf <- data.frame(formula="DELTARP ~ timepoint", family="t.test.paired_timepoint",                   transformation_y="none", depth_analysis=3,                   filter_p_value=TRUE)  # Kruskal-Wallis (> 2 groups, non-parametric ANOVA) inf <- data.frame(formula="MUTATIONS_HYPO ~ disease_stage", family="kruskal.test",                   transformation_y="none", depth_analysis=2,                   filter_p_value=TRUE)  # Chi-squared test (categorical dependent variable) inf <- data.frame(formula="MUTATIONS_HYPO ~ exposure", family="chisq.test",                   transformation_y="none", depth_analysis=1,                   filter_p_value=TRUE)  # Fisher's exact test (small samples, 2×2 tables) inf <- data.frame(formula="MUTATIONS_HYPO ~ binary_exposure", family="fisher.test",                   transformation_y="none", depth_analysis=1,                   filter_p_value=TRUE)  # Bartlett's test for equality of variances inf <- data.frame(formula="DELTARP ~ Sample_Group", family="bartlett.test",                   transformation_y="none", depth_analysis=2,                   filter_p_value=TRUE)  # Mean permutation test (non-parametric, permutation-based) # Syntax: "mean-permutation_<n_permutations>" inf <- data.frame(formula="DELTARP ~ Sample_Group", family="mean-permutation_1000",                   transformation_y="none", depth_analysis=3,                   filter_p_value=TRUE)  # Uncomment when you have a real semseeker results folder: # association_analysis(inf, result_folder = "~/semseeker_results/")

title: Association analysis — statistical models, correlation and group tests
text: GLMs (McCullagh Nelder 1989) appropriate relationship SEM metric clinical variable expected linear (Gaussian), outcome count (Poisson), predicting binary endpoint SEM scores (Binomial).
code: # Gaussian linear regression (continuous outcome) # Formula can include multiple predictors inf <- data.frame(formula="DELTARP ~ age + sex + bmi",                   family="gaussian",                   transformation_y="log",                   depth_analysis=3,                   filter_p_value=TRUE,                   covariates="sex,bmi")   # declare covariates explicitly  # Poisson regression (count data — mutations, lesions) inf <- data.frame(formula="MUTATIONS_HYPO ~ exposure_level",                   family="poisson",                   transformation_y="none",                   depth_analysis=2,                   filter_p_value=TRUE)  # Binomial / logistic regression (binary outcome) inf <- data.frame(formula="case_control ~ DELTARP",                   family="binomial",                   transformation_y="none",                   depth_analysis=3,                   filter_p_value=TRUE)  # Multinomial regression (> 2 categories) inf <- data.frame(formula="disease_stage ~ DELTARP",                   family="multinomial",                   transformation_y="none",                   depth_analysis=3,                   filter_p_value=TRUE)  # Uncomment when you have a real semseeker results folder: # association_analysis(inf, result_folder = "~/semseeker_results/")

title: Association analysis — statistical models, correlation and group tests
text: Quantile regression (Koenker 2005; Geraci Bottai 2014) particularly useful SEM metrics: unlike mean regression robust outliers captures full conditional distribution, matters high-quantile epigenetic variability biologically relevant.
code: # Standard quantile regression at quantile τ # Syntax: "quantreg_<tau>" inf <- data.frame(formula="DELTARP ~ age",                   family="quantreg_0.5",       # median regression                   transformation_y="none",                   depth_analysis=3,                   filter_p_value=TRUE)  # Quantile regression with permutation-based inference # Syntax: "quantreg-permutation_<tau>_<n_permutations>" inf <- data.frame(formula="DELTARP ~ age",                   family="quantreg-permutation_0.5_1000",                   transformation_y="none",                   depth_analysis=3,                   filter_p_value=TRUE)  # Quantile permutation model # Syntax: "quantile-permutation_<n_permutations>" inf <- data.frame(formula="DELTARP ~ Sample_Group",                   family="quantile-permutation_1000",                   transformation_y="none",                   depth_analysis=2,                   filter_p_value=TRUE)  # Uncomment when you have a real semseeker results folder: # association_analysis(inf, result_folder = "~/semseeker_results/")

title: Association analysis — statistical models, correlation and group tests
code: # Log₁₀ dose-response model # Syntax: "log10_<scale_factor>" inf <- data.frame(formula="DELTARP ~ dose",                   family="log10_1",                   transformation_y="none",                   depth_analysis=3,                   filter_p_value=TRUE)  # Power of 10 model inf <- data.frame(formula="DELTARP ~ dose",                   family="pow10_1",                   transformation_y="none",                   depth_analysis=3,                   filter_p_value=TRUE)  # Natural log model inf <- data.frame(formula="DELTARP ~ concentration",                   family="log_1",                   transformation_y="none",                   depth_analysis=3,                   filter_p_value=TRUE)  # Uncomment when you have a real semseeker results folder: # association_analysis(inf, result_folder = "~/semseeker_results/")

title: Association analysis — statistical models, correlation and group tests
code: # Syntax: "polynomial_<degree>" inf <- data.frame(formula="DELTARP ~ age",                   family="polynomial_2",       # quadratic                   transformation_y="none",                   depth_analysis=3,                   filter_p_value=TRUE)  # Uncomment when you have a real semseeker results folder: # association_analysis(inf, result_folder = "~/semseeker_results/")

title: Association analysis — statistical models, correlation and group tests
text: Mediation models test whether third variable (mediator) explains relationship SEM metric outcome:
code: # Mediation via quantile regression inf <- data.frame(formula="DELTARP ~ exposure + mediator",                   family="mediation-quantreg_0.5",                   transformation_y="none",                   depth_analysis=3,                   filter_p_value=TRUE)  # Mediation via ridge regression (regularised, handles multicollinearity) inf <- data.frame(formula="DELTARP ~ exposure + mediator",                   family="mediation-ridge",                   transformation_y="none",                   depth_analysis=3,                   filter_p_value=TRUE)  # Mediation via linear model inf <- data.frame(formula="DELTARP ~ exposure + mediator",                   family="mediation-linear",                   transformation_y="none",                   depth_analysis=3,                   filter_p_value=TRUE)  # Uncomment when you have a real semseeker results folder: # association_analysis(inf, result_folder = "~/semseeker_results/")

title: Association analysis — statistical models, correlation and group tests
text: Include covariates directly formula declare covariates column semseeker correctly separates independent variable interest confounders: collinearity_check = TRUE, semseeker automatically detects removes collinear covariates using VIF fitting model. covariates_pca = TRUE, covariates replaced principal components fitting.
code: inf <- data.frame(   formula          = "DELTARP ~ tcdd_exposure + age + sex + bmi",   family           = "gaussian",   transformation_y = "log",   depth_analysis   = 3,   filter_p_value   = TRUE,   covariates       = "age,sex,bmi",        # confounders to adjust for   collinearity_check = TRUE,               # auto-remove collinear covariates   covariates_pca   = FALSE                 # TRUE = replace with PCs )  # Uncomment when you have a real semseeker results folder: # association_analysis(inf, result_folder = "~/semseeker_results/")

title: Association analysis — statistical models, correlation and group tests
text: Run analysis subset samples using SQL clause:
code: # Only Case samples inf <- data.frame(   formula               = "DELTARP ~ age",   family                = "spearman",   transformation_y      = "none",   depth_analysis        = 3,   filter_p_value        = TRUE,   samples_sql_condition = "Sample_Group = 'Case'" )  # Only samples with age > 40 inf <- data.frame(   formula               = "DELTARP ~ bmi",   family                = "pearson",   transformation_y      = "none",   depth_analysis        = 3,   filter_p_value        = TRUE,   samples_sql_condition = "age > 40" )  # Uncomment when you have a real semseeker results folder: # association_analysis(inf, result_folder = "~/semseeker_results/")

title: Association analysis — statistical models, correlation and group tests
text: Combine multiple rows inference_details run analyses single call:
code: inference_details <- rbind(   data.frame(formula="DELTARP ~ age",             family="spearman",              transformation_y="none",             depth_analysis=3,              filter_p_value=TRUE,                 covariates=NA_character_,              stringsAsFactors=FALSE),   data.frame(formula="MUTATIONS_HYPO ~ Sample_Group", family="wilcoxon",              transformation_y="none",             depth_analysis=2,              filter_p_value=TRUE,                 covariates=NA_character_,              stringsAsFactors=FALSE),   data.frame(formula="DELTARP ~ bmi",             family="quantreg_0.5",              transformation_y="log",              depth_analysis=3,              filter_p_value=TRUE,                 covariates=NA_character_,              stringsAsFactors=FALSE),   data.frame(formula="MUTATIONS_HYPO ~ exposure + age + sex", family="poisson",              transformation_y="none",             depth_analysis=3,              filter_p_value=TRUE,                 covariates="age,sex",              stringsAsFactors=FALSE) )  # Uncomment when you have a real semseeker results folder: # association_analysis( #   inference_details = inference_details, #   result_folder     = "~/semseeker_results/", #   parallel_strategy = "multicore", #   maxResources      = 90 # )

title: Association analysis — statistical models, correlation and group tests
text: association_analysis() reports frequentist p-values / q-values region, assoc_bayes_analysis() provides probabilistic complement: per-region posterior probabilities computed via empirical Bayes. (MARKER, AREA, SUBAREA, AREA_OF_TEST) estimates: P(case  | epimutated) — likely sample case given SEM hit P(control | -epimutated) — likely sample control given absence SEM hit region reported candidate biomarker posteriors exceed configured thresholds (bayes_case_threshold bayes_control_threshold). Output written CSV files <result_folder>/Euristic/, sorted posterior probability. useful frequentist q-value ranking similar across many regions want probability-based criterion triage candidates. two analyses independent — running gives frequentist Bayesian view data.
code: assoc_bayes_analysis(   result_folder           = "~/semseeker_results/",   independent_variable    = "Sample_Group",   bayes_case_threshold    = 0.9,   # min P(case | epimutated)   bayes_control_threshold = 0.1,   # max P(control | epimutated)   parallel_strategy       = "multicore",   maxResources            = 90 )

title: Getting started — epimutations and delta metrics
text: semseeker R package detecting, quantifying analysing Stochastic Epigenetic Mutations (SEMs) Illumina array-based DNA methylation data (Corsaro 2025).

title: Getting started — epimutations and delta metrics
text: Stochastic Epigenetic Mutation rare, sample-specific aberration DNA methylation single CpG site — probe deviates population-level signal individual sample beyond can explained normal biological technical variability (Gentilini et al. 2015). SEMs first formally defined quantified Gentilini et al. (2015), showed SEM burden increases exponentially age (Gentilini et al. 2015). Subsequent work (Corsaro et al. 2023; Spada et al. 2020) extended framework disease association studies, introducing concept lesions (genomic clusters co-occurring SEMs) delta metrics population-level variability summaries. semseeker implements methodology wraps fully reproducible R workflow, raw beta-value matrix association analysis pathway enrichment (Corsaro 2025; Corsaro et al. 2023).

title: Getting started — epimutations and delta metrics
text: Starting normalised beta-value (M-value) matrix, semseeker: Derives signal thresholds (IQR-based) per probe reference population. Identifies hypomethylated hypermethylated SEMs single-probe resolution. Aggregates adjacent SEMs lesions (genomic clusters co-occurring mutations). Computes per-region delta metrics: signed deviation beyond reference interval (DELTAS), ratio interval width (DELTAR), binned/ranked variants (DELTAP/DELTARP, DELTAQ/DELTARQ). Optionally analyses raw methylation signal directly (mean per region, SIGNAL_MEAN) differential methylation without mutation calling. Supported input platforms: Illumina EPIC (850k), 450k, 27k arrays (detected automatically probe IDs).

title: Getting started — epimutations and delta metrics
text: semseeker expects probe × sample matrix beta values (0–1) M-values. Probe IDs must Illumina CpG identifiers (e.g. cg00000029). starting raw IDAT files, normalise first ChAMP:
code: # Example: load a pre-normalised matrix (rows = probes, columns = samples). # Here we use the small synthetic matrix prepared in the hidden setup # chunk above; in practice you would readRDS("normalised_beta.rds").  dim(beta_matrix)       # e.g. 866895 × 50 on a real EPIC dataset #> [1] 77 10 beta_matrix[1:3, 1:3] #>               CTRL01    CTRL02    CTRL03 #> cg11753499 0.4156308 0.3945168 0.4139144 #> cg13210239 0.6379258 0.6371134 0.6563844 #> cg17985533 0.5566887 0.5739348 0.5200354 library(ChAMP)  myLoad <- champ.load(   directory  = "path/to/idat/",   arraytype  = "EPIC" )  myNorm <- champ.norm(   beta      = myLoad$beta,   rgSet     = myLoad$rgSet,   mset      = myLoad$mset,   method    = "SWAN",   arraytype = "EPIC" )  beta_matrix <- myNorm

title: Getting started — epimutations and delta metrics
text: ISLAND subareas mirror Illumina’s Relation_to_Island, classifies every probe one six mutually exclusive categories. SEMseeker maps follows — , exactly GENE_WHOLE covers whole gene, ISLAND_WHOLE covers whole island neighbourhood: Notes: WHOLE = ISLAND + shores + shelves (whole CpG-island neighbourhood, island core ±4 kb). island core alone — use ISLAND core. OPENSEA CpGs outside every neighbourhood. open-sea probe grouped genomic gap neighbourhoods contains , labelled gap’s coordinate chr:start-end. gap never crosses chromosome boundary, open-sea region spans chromosomes. definitions apply WGBS / long-read data, island neighbourhoods reconstructed UCSC CpG-island coordinates instead Illumina manifest, array sequencing analyses share identical region semantics.

title: Getting started — epimutations and delta metrics
text: Beyond mutation calling, semseeker also tracks raw methylation signal per region via SIGNAL_MEAN marker: pivot table exactly structure mutation/delta tables can passed directly association_analysis() — allowing test whether mean methylation differs phenotype, without mutation-calling step. particularly useful want detect differential methylation region level (classic EWAS-style analysis) alongside epimutation burden analysis, using single unified framework.
code: # inference_details specifies the PREDICTOR and the test — not a model formula. # `independent_variable` is the phenotype column, `family_test` the statistical # test. The dependent marker (SIGNAL_MEAN, MUTATIONS, …) is NOT named here: it # comes from the markers you ran through semseeker(); association_analysis() # iterates over them and reads the matching pivot tables internally. inference_details <- data.frame(   independent_variable = "Sample_Group",   family_test          = "wilcoxon",   # two independent groups, non-parametric   transformation_y     = "none",   depth_analysis       = 3,            # per genomic area (region-level / EWAS-style)   filter_p_value       = TRUE )  association_analysis(   inference_details = inference_details,   result_folder     = "~/semseeker_results/",   parallel_strategy = "multicore",   maxResources      = 90 )

title: Getting started — epimutations and delta metrics
text: runnable demo uses subset probes (around BWS-related imprinting DMRs KCNQ1OT1:TSS-DMR H19_IGF2:IG-DMR) vignette builds seconds CRAN/Bioconductor checks. representative real-world workflow: production analysis normalise IDAT files entire cohort, correct biological confounders (age, cell composition whole-blood samples), run semseeker full beta matrix. block — kept commented eval = FALSE reference — shows complete end--end pipeline run real GEO dataset Beckwith-Wiedemann GSE95486. Uncomment adapt study. Pre-processing checklist running semseeker(): Cohort normalisation (SWAN / BMIQ / Functional Normalisation) Detection-p filter, multi-hit/SNP/cross-reactive probe removal Age residualisation (mandatory cohort age range > years) Cell composition deconvolution (whole-blood — FlowSorted.Blood.EPIC) Batch / processing-date correction (known) Sample QC pass (detection rate, bead count, predicted-sex check) semseeker assumes upstream preprocessing already performed. Skipping steps inflates false-positive SEM rate — particularly older cohorts whole-blood studies.
code: # ─── Full pipeline from raw GEO data (~30-60 min on a laptop) ──────────────  ## Step 0 — Download IDAT files from GEO (e.g. BWS GSE95486) library(GEOquery) gse <- getGEO("GSE95486", GSEMatrix = FALSE, destdir = "./raw") GEOquery::getGEOSuppFiles("GSE95486", baseDir = "./raw", makeDirectory = TRUE) # (then extract the IDATs into ./raw/GSE95486/IDATs/)  ## Step 1 — Load & SWAN-normalise IDAT files via ChAMP library(ChAMP) myLoad <- champ.load(   directory      = "./raw/GSE95486/IDATs",   arraytype      = "EPIC",   filterDetP     = TRUE,   filterSNPs     = TRUE,   filterMultiHit = TRUE,   filterXY       = TRUE ) myNorm <- champ.norm(   beta       = myLoad$beta,   rgSet      = myLoad$rgSet,   mset       = myLoad$mset,   method     = "SWAN",   arraytype  = "EPIC",   cores      = parallel::detectCores() - 1L )  ## Step 2 — Age correction ##   Confounding by chronological age is the strongest known driver of SEM ##   burden (Gentilini et al., 2015 — SEMs accumulate exponentially with ##   age). Always regress age out before disease-vs-control comparison. library(limma) age_residuals <- removeBatchEffect(   myNorm,   covariates = sample_sheet$Age   # numeric, years )  ## Step 3 — Cell composition deconvolution (whole-blood samples ONLY) ##   In peripheral whole blood, methylation is strongly confounded by the ##   relative proportions of B cells, CD4/CD8 T, monocytes, NK and granulocytes. ##   For tissues other than whole blood, skip this step. library(FlowSorted.Blood.EPIC) cells <- estimateCellCounts2(   myLoad$rgSet,   compositeCellType = "Blood",   referencePlatform = "IlluminaHumanMethylationEPIC" ) # Regress out the cell-fraction covariates from the (age-residualised) matrix adjusted <- removeBatchEffect(   age_residuals,   covariates = cells$counts )  ## Step 4 — Run SEMseeker on the fully preprocessed matrix SEMseeker::semseeker(   input         = adjusted,   sample_sheet  = sample_sheet,   result_folder = "./bws_full_results/" ) # ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

title: Pathway and enrichment analysis
text: WebGestalt (Wang et al. 2017; Liao et al. 2019) runs -Representation Analysis (ORA) (Boyle et al. 2004) Gene Set Enrichment Analysis (GSEA) (Subramanian et al. 2005) Gene Ontology (Ashburner et al. 2000), KEGG (Kanehisa Goto 2000), Reactome (Jassal et al. 2020), databases.
code: library(semseeker)  # Run after association_analysis() — session is already active inference_details <- data.frame(   formula               = "DELTARP ~ age",   family                = "spearman",   transformation_y      = "none",   depth_analysis        = 3,   filter_p_value        = TRUE,   stringsAsFactors      = FALSE )  association_analysis(   inference_details = inference_details,   result_folder     = "~/semseeker_results/",   parallel_strategy = "multicore" )  # Gene Ontology ORA on Biological Process and Molecular Function enrich_WebGestalt(   study          = "my_study",   types          = c("BP", "MF"),       # GO namespaces: BP, MF, CC   enrich_methods = c("ORA"),            # "ORA" or "GSEA"   pvalue_column  = "PVALUE_ADJ_ALL_BH", # which association p-value to use   inference_detail = inference_details[1, ],   significance   = TRUE                 # only use significant genes )  # GSEA on KEGG pathways enrich_WebGestalt(   study          = "my_study",   types          = c("KEGG"),   enrich_methods = c("GSEA"),   pvalue_column  = "PVALUE_ADJ_ALL_BH",   inference_detail = inference_details[1, ],   significance   = TRUE )

title: semseeker — Stochastic Epigenetic Mutation Seeker
text: semseeker detects Stochastic Epigenetic Mutations (SEMs) — aberrant, sample-specific methylation changes deviate expected population signal. Starting normalised beta-value (M-value) matrix, semseeker identifies hypomethylated hypermethylated mutations single-probe resolution, aggregates lesions (genomic clusters co-occurring mutations), computes per-region delta metrics — signed deviation beyond reference interval (DELTAS), ratio interval width (DELTAR), equal-width quantile ranked variants (DELTAP/DELTARP, DELTAQ/DELTARQ) — quantify stochastic epigenetic variability per region. Supported input technologies: Illumina microarrays: EPIC (850k), 450k, 27k — detected automatically Bisulfite sequencing (WGBS / RRBS) long-read sequencing (ONT / PacBio): planned (see documents/requirements.md)

text: R 4.6’s base tcltk package linked XQuartz’s /opt/X11/lib/libX11.6.dylib install time. Plain SEMseeker usage (library(SEMseeker), semseeker(...), association_analysis(...)) load tcltk works without XQuartz. Bioconductor optional features (e.g. annotation chains pulling minfi / GEOquery) transitively load tcltk, without XQuartz load segfaults invalid permissions. avoid surprise: download installer https://www.xquartz.org/. logout/login may required path picked .

what: Step 1 — Load and normalise IDAT files with ChAMP
title: semseeker — Stochastic Epigenetic Mutation Seeker
code: library(ChAMP)  idat_folder  <- "~/source_idat/" result_folder <- "~/result/"  myLoad <- champ.load(   directory      = idat_folder,   method         = "minfi",   methValue      = "B",   autoimpute     = TRUE,   filterDetP     = TRUE,   ProbeCutoff    = 0,   SampleCutoff   = 0.1,   detPcut        = 0.01,   filterBeads    = TRUE,   beadCutoff     = 0.05,   filterNoCG     = TRUE,   filterSNPs     = TRUE,   filterMultiHit = TRUE,   filterXY       = TRUE,   arraytype      = "EPIC"   # or "450K" )  myNorm <- champ.norm(   beta       = myLoad$beta,   rgSet      = myLoad$rgSet,   mset       = myLoad$mset,   resultsDir = result_folder,   method     = "SWAN",   arraytype  = "EPIC",   cores      = parallel::detectCores() - 1 )  saveRDS(myNorm, "~/normalizedData.rds")

title: Cross-pipeline annotation concordance report (Illumina manifest vs WGBS) — anno_concordance_report
text: Concordance categories (informational, returned category column): "bundled" — pipelines use bundled data     (cytoband_hg19.rda, dmr_annotation.rda). Expected rate: 1.0. "txdb" — WGBS path uses TxDb UCSC knownGene     Illumina manifest uses RefSeq. probes map different     gene symbols two sources. "annotationhub" — WGBS path uses CpG island BED     AnnotationHub (UCSC). Expected near-identical     manifest boundary probes may shift across shore/shelf strata. Two concordance rates returned: concordance_rate_strict: label sets (split ;)     must match exactly (setequal). concordance_rate_intersection: non-empty set intersection     (least one shared gene / island two pipelines). NA-probes (missing pipelines) counted n_both_na excluded rate denominators avoid inflating score.

text: AI-107 (2026-06-09). Bioconductor external annotations sometimes encode multiple genes compact slash-separated form suffix tokens inherit prefix first token. naive `strsplit(s, "/")` loses prefix produces unintelligible single-letter digit-tokens:

title: Smart split of a multi-gene AREA name with prefix recovery — .anno_smart_split_area_name
text: character vector expansion. `s`   contain `"/"`, returns `s` unchanged length-1 vector.

title: Smart split of a multi-gene AREA name with prefix recovery — .anno_smart_split_area_name
text: `"HLA-/B/C"`  →  `c("HLA-", "B", "C")`        (BAD)   `"KRT8/18"`    →  `c("KRT8", "18")`             (BAD) function reconstructs missing prefix using one two heuristics, applied order: - Strategy 1: first token contains `-`, take everything     including LAST `-` prefix:        `"HLA-"` → prefix `"HLA-"`   - Strategy 2: otherwise, take leading run alphabetic     characters prefix:        `"KRT8"` → prefix `"KRT"` non-first token prefixed already start recovered prefix (avoids double-prepending inputs like `"HLA-/HLA-B"` `"HBA1/HBA2"`).

title: Smart split of a multi-gene AREA name with prefix recovery — .anno_smart_split_area_name
code: # Internal helper — not exported. Reach it via ::: so R CMD check --as-cran # can run the examples block under CheckExEnv (which only sees exports). SEMseeker:::.anno_smart_split_area_name("HLA-A/B/C")    # c("HLA-A","HLA-B","HLA-C") #> [1] "HLA-A" "HLA-B" "HLA-C" SEMseeker:::.anno_smart_split_area_name("HBA1/HBA2")    # c("HBA1","HBA2") #> [1] "HBA1" "HBA2" SEMseeker:::.anno_smart_split_area_name("KRT8/18")      # c("KRT8","KRT18") #> [1] "KRT8"  "KRT18" SEMseeker:::.anno_smart_split_area_name("TP53")         # "TP53" #> [1] "TP53" SEMseeker:::.anno_smart_split_area_name("GENE-A-B/C/D") # c("GENE-A-B","GENE-A-C","GENE-A-D") #> [1] "GENE-A-B" "GENE-A-C" "GENE-A-D"
